consolation: Sometimes we give other people’s words and reactions more weight than they really deserve. Mistakes at work happen to everyone and do not define our worth as professionals or as human beings. You have achievements, strengths and experience that speak for your competence and personal value.
solutions: Try to separate a constructive comment about your work from a judgment of you as a person. It may be that your manager simply pointed out something that needed to be corrected in the task — and that is a natural part of the work process.
Remind yourself of your past successes and situations where you handled tasks well. This helps you see the situation in perspective and not draw conclusions about yourself based on a single episode.
Focus on strengthening your self-esteem. The more confident you feel, the easier it is to receive work-related feedback without experiencing it as personal criticism.

distortion.description: Chosen because it turns a single incident (a mistake at work) into a sweeping judgment about yourself (“everyone thinks I’m a failure”).

negativeThought: It feels like everyone around me thinks I’m a failure.
adaptiveResponse: One mistake does not define me as a professional or as a person. I can learn from this situation and rely on my skills and past successes.
